benchmarks.v2: 5 models (OURS · BASE · GPT-5.5 · Claude · Gemini), grounded product condition, 100 held-out positions.
benchmarks.open: Same grounded treatment extended to 9 bigger open models + the v2 five, 100 held-out positions.

definitions.sound: Recommended move is in this position's Stockfish sound pool (non-blunder).
definitions.tier_appropriate: Pick equals THE human-findable sound move for the tier (src/teacher/tier_select.select_tier_move): beginner=highest-Maia sound move, advanced=sharpest sound move, intermediate=blend.
definitions.fabricated: Faithfulness verifier found >=1 false board fact in the coaching text.
definitions.ours_wins: OURS beats >=1 frontier model on the same grounded input: (a) OURS sound+tier-appropriate where the frontier model is not, or (b) OURS sound+faithful where the frontier model fabricates.

positions.facts:
VERIFIED FACTS — use ONLY these. Never mention a piece, square, capture, or threat that is not listed here.
- Side to move: Black.
- Pieces on the board — White: Kg1, Re1, Bc4, Na8, Pa2, Pb2, Pg2, Ph2, Pd4 | Black: Kg7, Rh8, Bf4, Bg4, Ne7, Pg6, Pa7, Pb7, Pf7, Ph7
- Undefended / attacked pieces: Black knight on e7, White knight on a8.
- What each candidate move concretely does:
    * Nf5 — knight to f5; attacks the pawn on d4.
    * Bd6 — bishop to d6; attacks the pawn on h2; defends the knight on e7.
    * Rc8 — rook to c8; attacks the knight on a8, the bishop on c4.
    * Nc6 — knight to c6; attacks the pawn on d4; defends the pawn on a7.
    * Bg5 — bishop to g5; defends the knight on e7.
    * Ng8 — knight to g8.

models.name: OURS · chess-coach-v2 (1.7B)
models.coaching: I'd play Nf5. Your idea is right: you want to challenge White’s center pawn. The problem is that after Nf5, White can simply play h3 and remove your active knight. Play Nf5 again, and if White recaptures with h3, you have at least traded off the knight on e7. Takeaway: When you challenge a center pawn, do not play it once; if it recaptures, look for a follow-up move that removes the recapturer.

models.name: GPT-5.5
models.coaching: I'd play Bd6. Your move didn’t solve the loose knight problem, while Bd6 calmly defends the knight on e7. It also makes your bishop more active by attacking the pawn on h2, so you improve safety and create pressure at the same time. Takeaway: When one of your pieces is undefended, look first for a move that protects it while also improving another piece.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. Your knight on e7 was hanging when you played g5, and that pawn move ignored the bigger picture — you had a chance to win material instead of pushing pawns. With Rc8 your rook lines up against both the stranded knight on a8 and the bishop on c4, so White can't defend everything at once and you'll come out ahead. Always look for moves that make a threat while your opponent's pieces are loose and undefended. Takeaway: When your opponent has stranded or undefended pieces, hunt them down before making quiet pawn moves.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. Pushing your g-pawn doesn't improve your pieces and slightly weakens your king's safety without a clear goal. Instead, bringing your rook into the game with Rc8 is a much stronger and more active plan. It takes control of the c-file and immediately creates problems for White by attacking both their bishop on c4 and their trapped knight on a8. Takeaway: Always look for ways to activate your sleeping pieces, especially when they can create immediate threats against your opponent's position.

models.name: BASE · Qwen3-1.7B (untuned)
models.coaching: I'd play Nf5. The student's mistake was not attacking the key pawn on d4 with a knight. By playing Nf5, you're putting pressure on the center and attacking the critical d4 square. The idea is to follow up with h3 to control the h-file and then develop the knight to d6 or Nd6. This move sets up a solid plan and prepares the player for the next few moves. Your mistake was not attacking the key pawn on d4. By playing Nf5, you're putting pressure on that square and setting up a good follow-up with h3. After that, you can develop the knight to d6 or Nd6, which will control the center and prepare for the next move. This move is a good start and gives you a clear plan to follow. Takeaway: Attack the key pawn on d4 with a knight, and set up a solid plan for the next few moves.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. This move attacks your opponent's undefended knight on a8 and their bishop on c4, forcing them to react. Instead of pushing a pawn (which didn't address immediate threats), focus on capturing free pieces when possible. After Rc8, if White moves their bishop, you can simply take the knight next turn. Takeaway: Always look for undefended pieces you can attack with your pieces.

models.name: DeepSeek-V3.2
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. Your move gave away free time; instead, use yours to attack something that can't defend itself. Here your rook can jump on c8, hitting both the knight on a8 and the bishop, and the white bishop is loose with no other defender. This attacks two pieces at once in a way that forces your opponent to react, turning a quiet move into a threat. Takeaway: When you have a free move, look for simple attacks that hit more than one of your opponent’s undefended pieces.

models.name: Gemma-3-27B-it
models.coaching: I'd play Bd6. Your move g5 wasn't terrible to think about – you correctly noticed your knight on e7 felt a little vulnerable! – but it doesn’t really *do* anything to improve your position and lets White continue to build their attack. Bd6, on the other hand, develops a piece to a good square, defends your knight, *and* puts some pressure on White's pawn on h2. This starts a plan of controlling the long diagonal and making White show how strong their attack really is. Takeaway: Before you move, double-check that your move either defends a piece, attacks an enemy piece, or improves the position of one of your pieces.

models.name: GLM-5
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. This move puts pressure on two White pieces at once — the knight on a8 and the bishop on c4 — which is exactly the kind of active play you want. Your move ...g5 weakened your king's safety without creating any real threats, so instead learn to ask: "what piece can I attack or improve right now?" Here, the rook belongs on an open file where it does useful work. Takeaway: When you have an open file, put your rook on it and make your opponent deal with pressure.

models.name: Kimi-K2.5
models.coaching: I'd play Nf5. Your move g5 pushed a pawn that doesn't help your development, and it actually weakens your king's safety without creating any real threat. Nf5 is a perfect beginner move because it develops your knight to an active, central square with a clear purpose: it eyes the d4 pawn and gets ready to jump into d6, where it will dominate the board. This follows the golden rule of openings—get your pieces out to good squares before pushing pawns. Takeaway: Develop knights toward the center before moving flank pawns; active pieces beat static pawn pushes.

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B
models.coaching: I'd play Nf5. This move is a good choice because it helps to attack the pawn on d4, which is a strong pawn in the center of the board. By playing Nf5, you're also developing your knight to a good square where it can influence the center and prepare for future moves. Your mistake, playing g5, didn't address the weaknesses on the board, but Nf5 does, and it's a move that you can understand and reuse in similar positions. Takeaway: Developing a piece to attack a strong central pawn is often a good idea, especially when it also helps to prepare for other moves.

models.name: Mistral-Large-3 (675B)
models.coaching: I'd play Rc8. You missed a great chance to grab the stranded White knight on a8—it’s completely trapped and has no squares. Instead of pushing the pawn (which just weakens your kingside and doesn’t help you win anything), move the rook to c8 to threaten to take that knight. This also keeps an eye on White’s bishop, so you’re attacking and defending at the same time. It’s a simple, powerful idea that beginners often overlook: always check for loose pieces before making random pawn moves. Takeaway: Before you move a pawn, ask: “Is there a hanging piece I can grab first?”

models.name: Qwen3-32B
models.coaching: I'd play Nf5. This move lets your knight jump to a strong, active square where it directly attacks the central pawn on d4, gaining immediate control of the board's heart. The student's g5 pushed weakened the kingside and led to losing material — instead, playing Nf5 keeps your pieces sharp and avoids rash pawn moves. Takeaway: Prioritize moving your knights to active central squares before making impulsive pawn breaks.

models.name: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B
models.coaching: I'd play Bd6. You’re right to want to protect your knight on e7, and moving your bishop to d6 does exactly that—while also putting pressure on White’s h2 pawn, which is hard to defend. Your last move, g5, weakened your king’s safety without a clear purpose, but Bd6 builds a solid plan: keep your pieces safe and look for ways to win material, like snapping up that lonely knight on a8 later. This move connects your pieces and gives you control instead of giving White room to attack. Takeaway: Always protect your pieces before pushing pawns near your king.

models.name: OURS · chess-coach-v2 (1.7B)
models.coaching: I'd play Nc3. Your knight on f3 is already active, so the more important thing is to defend the pawn on b5 and the pawn on d5. Play Nc3: it covers both pawns while keeping your queen on a1 available for central play. Takeaway: When you are defending two pawns, choose the square that covers both and keeps your queen active.

models.name: GPT-5.5
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Your move jumped forward before finishing king safety, so castling is the clean beginner habit here. After O-O, your king is safer and your rook is brought to f1, while the pawn on f2 is also defended; then you can calmly continue developing. Takeaway: When you have a promising position, secure your king before starting an attack.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Your knight jump on the kingside pushed a piece forward before your king was safe and your rook was still stuck in the corner, and it didn't actually accomplish anything — so instead, tuck your king away and connect your development. Castling brings your rook toward the center and gets your king off the open middle of the board, and after that you can calmly bring your last knight into play with a move like Nc3 to shore up your advanced pawns. Finish your setup first; the attacking chances will come once everything is working together. Takeaway: Get castled and your pieces coordinated before launching a knight into the enemy camp.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Jumping your knight forward with Nfg5 is a bit premature because you are launching an attack before your own house is in order. Instead, it is much safer to castle, which tucks your king away and connects your pieces for the middlegame. Once your king is safe and your rooks are ready, your attacks will be much stronger and less risky. Takeaway: Always prioritize getting your king safe and finishing development before launching an attack.

models.name: BASE · Qwen3-1.7B (untuned)
models.coaching: I'd play Qc1. The student's move Nfg5 was a mistake because it left the king in a vulnerable position and weakened the center. By playing Qc1, you defend the pawn on c4 and the bishop on h6, which are critical for controlling the center. This move also sets up a strong attack on the kingside and prepares for a future attack on the queen's side. Your mistake was not defending the key squares and leaving the king exposed. Qc1 is a smart move that fixes the immediate weakness and sets up a solid plan for the future. Takeaway: Always prioritize defending key squares and controlling the center to set up a strong position.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Castling gets your king to safety and connects your rooks, which is crucial before starting an attack. The knight move Nfg5 left your king exposed in the center - now that you're castled, you can focus on developing your other pieces like the knight to c3 next. Takeaway: Always prioritize king safety through castling before launching aggressive moves.

models.name: DeepSeek-V3.2
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Your knight move left your king stuck in the center, and the first rule of good openings is to get your king safe. Castling does exactly that—it tucks your king away while also connecting your rooks for the middlegame. After castling, your next thought can be to defend your advanced pawns with moves like a6 or Nc3. Takeaway: Castling early is a safe, fundamental step that makes your whole position more secure.

models.name: Gemma-3-27B-it

Castling gets your king to safety and connects your rooks, which is a really important part of building a strong position. Now you can start thinking about developing your pieces further and planning an attack, knowing your king is safe. Takeaway: Before launching an attack, make sure your own king is secure.

models.name: GLM-5
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. Your move Nfg5 left your king exposed in the center while chasing a piece that could simply move away. Castling brings your king to safety behind your pawns and connects your rooks—this should almost always be your priority before launching attacks. Once your king is safe, you can look for good squares for your knights. Takeaway: Castle early to secure your king before starting any attacking adventures.

models.name: Kimi-K2.5
models.coaching: I'd play O-O. You jumped your knight forward to g5, but that knight wasn't helping you finish development and the move leaves your king stuck in the center. Castling here gets your king to safety and connects your rooks, which is exactly what your position needs most. Once castled, you can improve your pieces with ideas like Nc3 or Qb2, putting pressure on weak spots while your king sleeps soundly. models.name: Llama-3.3-70B
models.coaching: I'd play Nc3. This move helps to support the pawns on b5 and d5, which is important because they are somewhat exposed. By playing Nc3, you're also preparing to develop other pieces and get your king to a safer position, which is a good plan to focus on, especially since your previous move didn't quite work out as intended. Now you can think about castling kingside and getting your rook into the game, which will help you control more of the board and potentially launch a attack on Black's position. Takeaway: When you're not sure what to do, focusing on supporting your pawns and preparing to develop your pieces can often lead to a solid and safe plan.

models.name: Qwen3-32B

models.name: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B

Right now, your rook on h1 is stuck, and your king is in the center where Black’s pieces can target it. models.name: OURS · chess-coach-v2 (1.7B)
models.coaching: I'd play c5. Your move had the right beginner idea: use the c-pawn to attack White’s b4 pawn. The key is to make sure your pawn move is not just a random pawn push. After c5, your pawn attacks b4, so White has to deal with that pressure. How to find it: When you are about to push a pawn, pause and ask: 'Does this pawn move attack something right now?' models.name: GPT-5.5
models.coaching: I'd play a5. It attacks the pawn on b4, just like your c5 idea, but it does it in the most direct and easy-to-understand way. If White replies b5, you have at least made White spend a move and fixed the queenside structure a bit more clearly. Your instinct to challenge b4 was good; choose the pawn move that makes that plan simplest. Takeaway: When two moves attack the same target, prefer the one that keeps your plan clearest and most direct.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.coaching: I'd play a5. Your idea of pushing on the queenside was actually right — the problem with c5 is that it lets White play in a way that leaves your pawns a bit loose. Instead, a5 hits the b4 pawn directly and asks White a question without weakening your own structure, and you keep your good bishop on b6 pointing at White's kingside. This is a cleaner way to open lines on the side where you have pressure. Takeaway: When you want to attack a pawn chain, push the pawn that challenges it without loosening your own pawns.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
models.coaching: I'd play a5. You correctly saw that White's queenside pawns are creeping forward and wanted to strike back. However, pushing your c-pawn can leave your central pawns a bit vulnerable and clogs up the center. Instead, pushing your a-pawn directly attacks White's advanced b4 pawn from the edge, safely challenging their space while potentially opening up the a-file for your rook to join the action. Takeaway: When your opponent pushes pawns on the wing, challenge them by striking with your own outside pawns to open lines for your rooks without weakening your center.

models.name: BASE · Qwen3-1.7B (untuned)
models.coaching: I'd play c5. The student's move was a mistake because it attacked the pawn on b4, but the real problem is that the position is very weak on the kingside. By playing c5, you're setting up a strong attack on the b4 pawn and also preparing to push the e5 square. The real issue is that the kingside is too weak, and the student didn't fix that. Instead of focusing on the b4 pawn, you should focus on strengthening the kingside and preparing for a counterattack. Takeaway: Focus on strengthening the kingside and preparing for a counterattack, not just attacking the weak pawns.

models.coaching: I'd play Bc7+. Your Nf3 move missed the more forcing chance: Bc7+ gives check on the king on d8, attacks the queen on h4, and defends your pawn on c2. That is the kind of active move you want to find: it uses your queen or bishop to make an immediate threat instead of just moving a piece somewhere safe. How to find it: When it is your turn, run this quick checklist before choosing a normal move: 'Can I give check, attack an important piece, or defend something while improving my piece?' Here, Bc7+ passes that test because it checks the king and defends c2 at the same time. Takeaway: Look for active moves that give check or attack an important piece before playing a quiet improving move.

models.name: GPT-5.5

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8

Instead, just notice that Black's knight on g4 is undefended and grab it for free with your h-pawn; this also keeps your f5-pawn protected. Even if Black grabs your rook in the corner afterward, you're up a whole piece and can chase the queen away next. Takeaway: Before anything fancy, look for an enemy piece that's simply hanging and take it.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro

By playing g3, you immediately attack the Black queen and force it to retreat or make a decision. Kicking the queen away with a supported pawn is a safe and solid way to relieve the pressure and start untangling your position. Takeaway: When your opponent's queen is aggressively placed near your king, look for safe pawn moves to kick it away and disrupt their attack.

The real mistake was not attacking the knight on g4, which is a critical weakness for Black. Instead, g3 is a solid, immediate threat that sets up future play. Castling allowed Black to take your pawn next move, but now you're protecting it while putting pressure on their king. After their king moves, you can follow up with f5 to gain space. Takeaway: Use checks to defend pieces while developing your queen to active squares.

models.name: DeepSeek-V3.2
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. You correctly wanted to get your king safe, but castling right now lets your f-pawn be captured for free. Instead, your queen can give a useful check while also defending that pawn. After the black king moves, you'll be ready to push your f-pawn forward next turn and start an attack. This is a simple, active way to protect a piece and develop your queen to a good square. Takeaway: Use checks to improve your piece's position while defending another threat.

models.name: Gemma-3-27B-it
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. You understandably wanted to get your king safe with castling, but sometimes a direct attack is better! Qg4+ immediately puts pressure on Black's king and forces a response, rather than letting Black develop their pieces comfortably. After Black plays Kb8, you can follow up with f5, opening up the position and creating more attacking opportunities. Takeaway: Before making a defensive move, always double-check if you have a simple, direct attack available.

models.name: GLM-5
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. This move gives check while doing two important jobs at once: it attacks Black's king and also protects your pawn on f4, which is currently under attack. Castling is normally a great move, but here it ignores the immediate threat to your f4 pawn, and you end up losing material needlessly. When you can give a check that also defends your pieces at the same time, you're usually doing something right. Takeaway: Look for moves that solve multiple problems at once, especially when they give check.

models.name: Kimi-K2.5
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. You castled into a position where your f4 pawn was already under pressure — that's the bug we need to fix. Instead, Qg4+ uses your queen actively: it checks the king, defends your loose pawn on f4, and protects g2 all in one move. Beginners often tuck their king away automatically, but here asking "what can my queen do right now?" finds a forcing, useful move. After the king moves, you've gained time to keep developing with threats. Takeaway: Before castling, ask if your queen can make a forcing move that solves problems and improves your position.

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. This move is a good choice because it addresses the issue of the undefended pawn on f4 by defending it, while also putting pressure on Black's position with a check. By playing Qg4+, you're taking control of the situation and preparing to develop other pieces, which is a key part of improving your position. This move also helps you to think about how to use your pieces to support each other and defend weak points, which is an important skill to develop as a beginner. Takeaway: When you have a weak point, like an undefended pawn, look for moves that defend it while also putting pressure on your opponent.

models.name: Mistral-Large-3 (675B)
models.coaching: I'd play f5. You noticed the pawn on f4 is under attack and knew you needed to do something about it. Instead of just tucking your king away with O-O, f5 blocks the queen’s access to your pawn and sets up a pawn push that can open the center for your pieces. It also stops Black from playing ...d5, which would free their knight and queen. This move keeps the pressure on while defending actively—something beginners often miss when they focus only on "safety first" moves like castling. Takeaway: When a pawn is threatened, look for ways to defend it by improving your position—not just by running away.

models.name: Qwen3-32B
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. This check not only puts pressure on Black's exposed king but also helps defend your f4 pawn and prepares for a possible pawn push with f5, which can take initiative in the center. Instead of castling and passively moving the rook, you're taking control of the game and limiting your opponent's space. Takeaway: Look for checks—they can be powerful tools to limit your opponent’s options and take control of the game.

models.name: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B
models.coaching: I'd play Qg4+. You castled, which is great for safety, but you left your f-pawn hanging and didn’t address the threat. Qg4+ forces the black king to move, while also protecting your f-pawn and putting pressure on their g-pawn — it’s like putting two problems at once in front of your opponent. This move helps you take control instead of waiting for them to strike. You’re not just defending; you’re making them react. Takeaway: Always look for checks that defend your weak pieces — they’re powerful and easy to spot.

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B

models.name: Mistral-Large-3 (675B)

Even better, that developed bishop now attacks the rook on a1, so you solve a defensive issue and create a clear follow-up plan at the same time. Takeaway: When under pressure, prefer a move that improves a piece while fixing a loose piece.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.coaching: I'd play Bxg7. The check with Qb4+ looks tempting, but it doesn't address the real problem: that bishop sitting on g7 is forking your rook on h8 and eyeing your position, and your own bishop on f8 is loose. By capturing on g7 you snap off White's active bishop, defend your rook on h8, and even hit the rook on a1 — solving your problems while creating a threat of your own. After White recaptures, notice you have Nb4, bringing your knight into the game with tempo. Takeaway: Before playing a flashy check, first deal with the pieces that are actually hanging or attacking you — solving the concrete problem usually beats chasing the enemy king.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
models.coaching: I'd play Bxg7. Your move, Qb4+, throws in a check, but it ignores the massive problems on your kingside where White's bishops are causing havoc. By simply capturing the bishop with Bxg7, you eliminate one of White's most dangerous attacking pieces, defend your h8 rook, and start untangling your position. When you have severe threats to address, don't get distracted by a check that doesn't improve your overall coordination. Takeaway: Always address immediate threats and eliminate dangerous enemy pieces before giving in to the temptation of a random check.

models.name: BASE · Qwen3-1.7B (untuned)
models.coaching: I'd play Bxg7. The student missed a critical threat that could win the game. By capturing the bishop on g7, you remove a big piece from the board and put pressure on the king. The move also opens up a way to attack the rook on a1. After this, you can follow up with Qxg7 or Nb4 to continue the attack. The mistake was not seeing that capturing the bishop gives a big advantage and sets up a strong follow-up. Instead of focusing on the queen move, you should prioritize the immediate threat and set up for future wins. Takeaway: Capturing a bishop can be a powerful first move that opens up multiple attacking lines.

positions.facts:
VERIFIED FACTS — use ONLY these. Never mention a piece, square, capture, or threat that is not listed here.
- Side to move: White.
- Pieces on the board — White: Kc1, Rh1, Bf1, Bb4, Nd5, Nh8, Pa2, Pb2, Pf2, Pg2, Ph2, Pe4 | Black: Ke6, Ra8, Bc2, Nd4, Nh6, Pa7, Pb7, Pc7, Pg7, Ph7
- Undefended / attacked pieces: White knight on h8, White pawn on e4, Black pawn on c7.
- What each candidate move concretely does:
    * Nxc7+ — knight captures the pawn on c7; gives check; attacks the rook on a8, the king on e6.
    * Bc3 — bishop to c3; attacks the knight on d4; defends the pawn on b2.
    * Bc5 — bishop to c5; attacks the pawn on a7, the knight on d4.
    * Bc4 — bishop to c4; develops a minor piece; defends the pawn on a2, the knight on d5.
    * Ng6 — knight to g6.

models.name: OURS · chess-coach-v2 (1.7B)
models.coaching: I'd play Nxc7+. Your Ng6 idea is understandable because it develops a knight and defends the pawn on a2, but it misses the more forcing move: your active knight can take the undefended c7 pawn while also giving check on the king on e6. After Nxc7+, your knight attacks the rook on a8 and defends the pawn on b2, so this is not just a capture — it improves your position while creating a concrete problem for Black. Takeaway: When you have an active piece, look for a forcing capture before a quiet developing move.

models.name: GPT-5.5
models.coaching: I'd play Nxc7+. This is the forcing version of your idea: the knight captures the pawn on c7 with check and attacks the rook on a8, so Black has to answer your check before dealing with the rook. Compared with Ng6, this uses tempo much better—you create an immediate problem for the king and a concrete follow-up like Nxa8. Takeaway: When you have a forcing check that also attacks material, look at it before quieter knight moves.

models.name: Claude Opus 4.8
models.coaching: I'd play Nxc7+. Your Ng6 was trying to save the stranded knight on h8, but it hands Black a free tempo — after ...hxg6 you've traded your active plan for a passive one. Instead, play the forcing fork: the knight checks the king and simultaneously attacks the rook on a8, so after the king steps away you simply grab the rook with Nxa8, winning material outright. That knight on h8 was already loose, so don't try to rescue it when you have a stronger tactic that wins the exchange. Takeaway: Before rescuing a loose piece, check whether a forcing check or fork wins even more material elsewhere.

models.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro
models.coaching: I'd play Nxc7+. Your move Ng6 was a creative attempt to rescue your trapped knight on h8, but it misses a massive immediate opportunity. By jumping to c7 with your d5 knight, you deliver a devastating fork on the black king and the rook on a8. Sometimes, letting a doomed piece go is the best choice when you can win far more material on the other side of the board. Takeaway: Always scan for forcing tactical shots like checks and forks before spending time trying to rescue a trapped piece.

models.name: BASE · Qwen3-1.7B (untuned)
models.coaching: I'd play Bc4. The student's move Ng6 was a mistake because it left the king in a vulnerable position and gave the opponent a clear attack on the king. By playing Bc4, you develop the bishop and defend the a2 pawn, which is crucial for long-term play. This move also sets up a good plan to attack the king and create a strong follow-up. Bc4 is a solid development that prepares for a future attack on the king and strengthens the position. It’s a move that forces the opponent to respond, and it’s a key step in controlling the center and attacking the king. Takeaway: Control the center and develop your pieces to attack the king, not just focus on immediate threats.
